coredata.dc:description: The extraction of photogenerated holes is of great significance for the improvement of both the efficiency and stability of photocatalysts. In this work, carbon dots (CDs) were used to modify the hole transporting ligand ferrocenecarboxylic acid (FcA) by simple hydrothermal treatment to obtain CDs-FcA hybrids, which were further combined with Ag-In-Zn-S (AIZS) quantum dots (QDs) to form AIZS-CDs-FcA composites by in-situ synthesis. CDs act as an effective hole transfer intermediate between FcA and AIZS QDs. The optimal hydrogen production rate reached 2.3 mmol g−1 h-1 with 4 % CDs and 0.64 % FcA, which is 5.8 and 2.9 times to that of AIZS QDs and AIZS-FcA, respectively. Moreover, with ascorbic acid as the hole sacrificial agent, time-resolved photoluminescence study indicates a dramatically boosted hole transfer rate in AIZS-CDs-FcA (1.97 × 1010 s-1 mM-1), compared to AIZS QDs (1.11 × 108 s-1 mM-1) and AIZS-FcA (7.28 × 108 s-1 mM-1). When 2.5 % Ni2+ was further added for electron extraction, the hydrogen production rate reached 13.1 mmol g-1 h-1 under the synergistic electron/hole extraction, achieving a 31.9-fold enhancement compared to pure AIZS QDs. This work points out for the first time the critical role of CDs as hole transfer mediator and shines light on the design of novel hole extraction reagents for photocatalysis employing the charge accumulation characteristic of carbon-based materials.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots are used as a photocatalytic hole transfer mediator for the first time, resulting in two-orders higher hole transfer rate and a 31 times enhancement of hydrogen production rate in AIZS-CDs-FcA.

The extraction of photogenerated holes is of great significance for the improvement of both the efficiency and stability of photocatalysts. In this work, carbon dots (CDs) were used to modify the hole transporting ligand ferrocenecarboxylic acid (FcA) by simple hydrothermal treatment to obtain CDs-FcA hybrids, which were further combined with Ag-In-Zn-S (AIZS) quantum dots (QDs) to form AIZS-CDs-FcA composites by in-situ synthesis. CDs act as an effective hole transfer intermediate between FcA and AIZS QDs. The optimal hydrogen production rate reached 2.3 mmol g−1 h-1 with 4 % CDs and 0.64 % FcA, which is 5.8 and 2.9 times to that of AIZS QDs and AIZS-FcA, respectively. Moreover, with ascorbic acid as the hole sacrificial agent, time-resolved photoluminescence study indicates a dramatically boosted hole transfer rate in AIZS-CDs-FcA (1.97 × 1010 s-1 mM-1), compared to AIZS QDs (1.11 × 108 s-1 mM-1) and AIZS-FcA (7.28 × 108 s-1 mM-1). When 2.5 % Ni2+ was further added for electron extraction, the hydrogen production rate reached 13.1 mmol g-1 h-1 under the synergistic electron/hole extraction, achieving a 31.9-fold enhancement compared to pure AIZS QDs. This work points out for the first time the critical role of CDs as hole transfer mediator and shines light on the design of novel hole extraction reagents for photocatalysis employing the charge accumulation characteristic of carbon-based materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic water splitting has been considered as one of the most ideal methods for producing hydrogen by directly utilizing the continuous solar energy and water [1–3]. However, the visible light utilization is still far below expected, which accounts for nearly 43 % of the sunlight and plays a critical role in better use of solar energy [4,5]. Quantum dots (QDs) are considered to be the most promising visible-active photocatalyst candidates due to their unique quantum confinement effect, ideal optical properties and large specific surface area [6–8]. Compared to traditional IIVI QDs, Cd-free I-III-VI QDs attracted tremendous attention in photocatalysis because of their widely adjustable band gap via composition and low toxicity, making them one class of the most promising visible-active candidates [9,10]. However, the hydrogen production efficiency and the stability of I-III-VI QD photocatalysts are still not as high as expected, partly due to the poor charge separation ability and subsequent photo- corrosion in I-III-VI QDs [11–14]. It has been more and more realized that improving the efficiency of charge separation is the key for the above shortcomings, for which various strategies have been employed, such as heterostructure design [15], defect engineering [16,17] and cocatalysts loading [18,19].

Among these strategies, boosting hole transfer is one of the most important means to improve the efficiency of charge separation [20,21]. However, hole transfer related research is kind of neglected in I-III-VI photocatalysts. In photocatalytic hydrogen production studies, a popular way to improve the charge separation is choosing different hole sacrificial agents, such as methanol and triethanolamines [22]. Strong hole sacrificial agents, such as sulfide/sulfite and ascorbic acid, are more preferred during activity estimation of metal sulfide photocatalysts to avoid photo-corrosion [23–25]. Another important method is to modify the photocatalysts with organic hole transporting ligands with high hole transfer rate to improve the extraction efficiency. Hole-transporting materials used in photovoltaics, such as polyethoxythiophene [26–28], thiophene [29], phenothiazine, ferrocene [30–32] and their derivatives, have been widely explored in photocatalysis to combine with CdS and CdSe etc. However, the direct modification of organic hole transporting ligands on photocatalysts cannot guarantee an excellent transport rate. The main reasons include the energy level mismatch and possibly the small contact area between each other. Regarding, this phenomenon, there is an urgent need to explore adding intermediate transporting mediums between the organic hole transporting ligands and the catalysts.

In photocatalysis studies, cocatalysts play another important role in charge separation [19,33–35]. Carbon dots (CDs) have been widely studied as an merging class of nano-carbon material with unique optoelectronic properties, good water solubility, and high stability [36]. Due to their unique optoelectronic characteristics, CDs have been widely used as various functional components in photocatalysts, such as photosensitizers, electron acceptors and electron storage mediators [37–40]. Among them, CDs were mainly used to promote light absorption and reduce charge recombination [41]. In the g-C3N4/NCDs/MoS2 ternary heterojunction using N-doped CDs, MoS2 acts as an electron extractor and g-C3N4/NCDS form a Z-type heterojunction to promote charge transfer [42]. It worth to mention that CDs mainly act as electron acceptors and charge storage mediators in photocatalysis. However, there is very few report on CDs acting as hole acceptors [43] and no clear demonstration of CDs’ role as efficient hole transfer agents. Considering the multiple energy levels in CDs, along with the critical role of band alignment in photocatalyst design, CDs are expected to make a unique contribution as hole transporting material with tunable highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) levels to achieve efficient hole transfer.

In view of the above analysis, here we developed a simple hydrothermal method to connect the hole accepting ligand (ferrocene formic acid, FcA) with CDs for the first time towards better hole extracting capability. The CDs-FcA hybrid was then combined with Ag-In-Zn-S (AIZS) QDs to construct a ternary AIZS-CDs-FcA composite via in-situ growth [44,45]. After systematic optimization, the photocatalytic hydrogen production rate of AIZS-CDs-FcA reached as high as 2.3 mmol g−1 h−1, 5.8 and 2.9 times higher than that of AIZS QDs and AIZS-FcA, respectively. Moreover, by further incorporating Ni2+ as an electron co-catalyst for synergetic electron/hole extraction, the hydrogen production rate was dramatically increased to 13.1 mmol g−1 h−1, giving a total of 31.9-fold promotion compared to the pristine QDs. This can be attributed to the fact that CDs-FcA act as a much more efficient hole acceptor in AIZS-CDs-FcA composites due to the multiple energy levels of CDs. Moreover, the hole transfer rates were measured for the first time through a series of time-resolved photoluminescence(PL) lifetime tests, indicating two orders of increase from AIZS QDs (1.11 × 108 s−1 mM−1), AIZS-FcA (7.28 × 108 s−1 mM−1) to AIZS-CDs-FcA (1.97 × 1010 s−1 mM−1).

Silver nitrate, zinc acetate, indium nitrate, thioacetamide, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), l-cysteine, ascorbic acid, ferrocenecarboxylic acid (FcA) and graphite rod were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs and AIZS QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthetic methods of CDs (electrochemical etching) and AIZS QDs (hydrothermal method) are the same as that reported in the literature [46,47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modification of AIZS QDs with FcA
ce:section.ce:para: 0.5 mg of FcA was first dissolved in 500 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide to form a clear solution (1 mg/mL), which was mixed with 10 mL of the AIZS QDs dispersion in water (10 mg/mL) and sonicated for 2 min to form a uniform transparent solution. Finally, the mixture contained in a glass vial was placed in a 35 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and kept in an oven at 110 ℃ for 4 h. AIZS-FcA composite was also obtained by using the in-situ method, where FcA was introduced during the synthesis of AIZS QDs, which gave similar results to the post-modification method.

A certain amount of FcA solution in dimethyl sulfoxide solution (1 mg/mL) and 10 ml of CDs aqueous solution (1 mg/mL) were mixed in glass vial and sonicated for 2 min to form a homogeneous solution. The mixed solution was put into a 35 ml Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and reacted at 140 °C for 4 h. Keeping the reaction temperature and time unchanged, the mass fractions of FcA to CDs were tuned from 4%, 7%, 10 %, 13 %, 16 %, 19% to 22%, respectively.

In-situ synthesis of AIZS-CDs-FcA composites was carried out by hydrothermal treatment. Specifically, silver nitrate (0.0576 g), indium nitrate (0.649 g) and zinc acetate (0.186 g)) in 5.5 mL distilled water were mixed with 6 ml of l-cysteine (0.6058g). When the pH of the mixed solution turned to 8.5, different amounts of CDs-FcA (3, 6, 9, 12 and 15 mg) were added. Finally, 6.5 ml of thioacetamide (0.244 g) solution was added and stirred for about 3−5 min, which was then kept at 110 °C for 4 h. After the reaction was completed, it was washed with ethanol and centrifuged at 9800 rpm for 5 min. Finally, the samples were dissolved in distilled water and stored for further use.

PIXcel3D X-ray diffractometer (Empyrean, Holland Panalytical) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.154178 nm) was used for X-ray diffraction (XRD). Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), high resolution TEM (HRTEM) and Scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) were used to characterize the samples using a Tecnai G2 F30 S-Twin (FEI) electron microscope with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The composition of the products was examined by a scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM-840A and LEO1530VP) equipped with an energy dispersive spectrometry (EDX). Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Nicolet Nexus 470) was used to test the infrared spectra of the samples. XPS measurements were conducted on a KRATOS Axis ultra-DLD X-ray photo-electron spectroscope (XPS) with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source (hυ =1283.3 eV). Cary 8450 was used for UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer for PL of the QDs. Time-resolved PL spectra were collected using a QuantaMaster™ 40 spectrometer (Photon Technology International, Inc.) excited at 481 nm. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of the samples was carried out with a PerkinElmer Pyris 6 in nitrogen atmosphere at a heating rate of 10 ℃ min−1. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was operated using a standard three lectrode system with CHI 920C workstation (CH Instruments, Inc., China). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were measured by a 760B electrochemical workstation (CH Instruments, Inc., China) with the samples well-distributed on FTO glass.

A 9-channel photocatalytic reactor (PCX50B Discover, Perfect Light) with white LED light sources was used for the photocatalytic hydrogen production test. The reaction solution was formed by dissolving fresh photocatalysts (20 mg) in 15 ml aqueous solution containing 0.25 M ascorbic acid as the sacrificial agent and transferring to a 40 ml reaction vial. Then, the mixed solution was with nitrogen for 15 min. The whole reaction process was continuously illuminated for 5 h. 1.5 mL of gas was taken out from the vial every hour and quickly injected into the gas chromatograph (GC7900, TCD detector) to detect the amount of hydrogen produced by the photocatalysts.

The process for synthesizing AIZS-CDs-FcA composite material is shown in Scheme 1 , where FcA or presynthesized CDs-FcA is used to in situ modify the AIZS QDs. Fig. S1a shows the TEM image of CDs. It can be clearly seen that CDs are spherical nanoparticles with an average size of about 2.4 ± 0.3 nm (Fig. S1b). From the XRD pattern of CDs (Fig. S1c), there are two obvious broad peaks near 26° and 43°, which are attributed to the (002) and (100) crystal planes of graphitic carbon. The Raman spectrum of CDs (Fig. S1d) has two peaks at 1335 and 1600 cm−1 that come from the D band of sp3 carbon in disordered carbonand the G band of sp2 carbon in graphite, respectively. The survey spectrum of XPS (Fig. S1e, f) shows that CDs contain both C and O elements. The C 1s high-resolution XPS spectrum of CDs can be fitted to three peaks with binding energies of 284.6, 286.2 and 288.5 eV, which correspond to the CC and COOH bonds. From the UV–vis absorption curve (Fig. S1g), CDs have a high absorbance in the visible range. Pure AIZS QDs were also synthesized for comparison, which are uniformly dispersed with average size of 3.8 ± 0.8 nm (Fig. S2a). As shown in Fig. S2b, AIZS-FcA shows slight agglomeration of multiple particles due to the induction of FcA, but the size of each QDs (3.5 ± 0.6 nm) still remains the same as that without FcA. Both of the HRTEM images of AIZS QDs (insets in Fig. S2a) and AIZS-FcA (insets in Fig. S2b) show a lattice fringe of 0.32 nm, which corresponds to the (310) plane of AIZS [48]. Fig. 1 a and b show the TEM and HRTEM images of the AIZS-CDs-FcA composite, respectively. It can be seen from the TEM image that more dark nanoparticles are connected together. The main reason is that the AIZS-CDs-FcA composites were obtained by in-situ hydrothermal synthesis. In the HRTEM image, it was found that two kinds of lattice fringes are closely connected, where 0.21 nm corresponds to the (100) plane of graphite [49] and 0.32 nm corresponds to the (310) plane of AIZS (Fig. 1b). This proves the successful preparation of the composite nanomaterials of AIZS and CDs-FcA. STEM and element mapping images (Fig. S4) further show the existence and uniform distribution of all elements in the AIZS-CDs-FcA composite and the uniform attachment of FcA adheres to the AIZS QDs. Fig. 1c shows the XRD patterns of AIZS QDs, AIZS-FcA and AIZS-CDs-FcA. Among them, AIZS QDs and AIZS-FcA have the same diffraction peaks, where the diffraction peaks at 27.3°, 46.5° and 55° correspond to the (311), (440) and (553) crystal planes of AgIn5S8 (JCPDS no. 25-1329), respectively. The diffraction peak positions of AIZS-CDs-FcA are approximately the same as AIZS QDs [48]. However, because CDs have a broad diffraction peak around 26°, which is close to the position of AIZS QDs (311) peak at 27.3° and results in a wider diffraction peak around 27° in AIZS-CDs-FcA. It is mainly composed of the 26.3° diffraction peaks of (002) plane in CDs and the 27.3° diffraction peak of (311) in AIZS QDs [50,51]. To further confirm the chemical composition and valence of the elements, XPS was performed on different samples. As shown in Fig. 1d & e, it can be seen that the binding energies in AIZS QDs can be ascribed to Ag+ (367.70 eV and 373.74 eV corresponding to Ag 3d5/2 and 3d3/2, respectively), and In3+ (444.70 eV and 452.25 eV corresponds to In 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 respectively). With the introduction of FcA and CDs-FcA, the XPS peaks of AIZS-FcA (Ag 3d5/2, 367.79 eV and 3d3/2, 373.79 eV; In 3d5/2, 444.80 eV and 3d3/2, 452.38 eV) move slightly toward higher binding energy than that of AIZS QDs, where the binding energies of AIZS-CDs-FcA (Ag 3d5/2, 367.90 eV and 3d3/2, 373.89 eV; In 3d5/2, 444.85 eV and 3d3/2, 452.40 eV) show even larger shifts. These relatively larger shifts of 0.1 eV for Ag 3d and 0.05 eV for In 3d indicated the strong influence of CDs-FcA, compared to the literature values with doping or heterojunction construction of AIZS [47,52]. Other elements, such Zn and S also show similar trend of blue shift towards larger binding energies (Figs. S3a and S3b). This blue shift indicates that there is a certain charge transfer at the interface of the AIZS-CDs-FcA composites [39]. This also shows that there is a strong interaction between AIZS QDs and CDs-FcA, which could thereby promote the transfer of photogenerated charge carriers. As shown in Fig. S3c, the N 1s XPS peak can be deconvoluted to 399.3 and 400 eV, which can be attributed to the N in amine (-NH2) and amide (-NH-C = O) groups in AIZS-CDs-FcA [53,54]. The formation of the amide group is due to the interaction of -NH2 in l-cysteine in AIZS QDs with the carboxylic group (−COOH) in CDs, which provides another important evidence for the strong coupling of AIZS QDs and CDs. Fig. 2 f is the Fe 2p XPS spectra of AIZS-FcA and AIZS-CDs-FcA, where the peaks at 703.9 and 716.4 eV correspond to Fe2+ 2p3/2 and 2p1/2, and 707.9 and 720.1 eV correspond to Fe3+ 2p3/2 and 2p1/2, respectively [55,56]. Ferric ions are derived from the Fe element in FcA. The main reason is that ferrocene has its own redox ability and Fe (III) was easily formed under the oxidation of the photogenerated holes [57]. Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) was further used to test the structure of the composites. As shown in Fig. S5, the absorption peak at 490 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of C-Fe, and the peaks at 650 cm−1 and 775 cm−1 are attributed to the CH stretching vibration of FcA. The absorption peaks at 1120 cm−1 and 1150 cm−1 are attributed to the CC stretching vibration of ferrocene. The peaks at 1389 cm−1 and 1610 cm−1 in AIZS-CDs-FcA and AIZS QDs is attributed to the OH and CO stretching vibration of l-cysteine [58]. As shown in Fig. S6, it is proved by EDS that the element proportion of Fe is 0.4 %, 0.48 % and 0.63 % in AIZS-CDs-0.4 % FcA, AIZS-CDs-0.52 % FcA and AIZS-CDs-0.64 % FcA, close to the initially added amount. As shown in Fig. S7, The TGA curve of AIZS-CDs-(0.88 %) FcA can be divided into three obvious steps and the total weight loss rate is 17.83 %. In the first stage, the 9 wt% reduction in mass between room temperature and 180 °C was due to the solvent loss. In the second stage, the mass reduction of 7.99 wt% between 200 and 600 ℃ was due to the decomposition of l-cysteine [59]. Among them, there is a 0.84 % weight loss process at 350–400 °C [60], which is mainly due to the decomposition of the lower content of FcA. The successful synthesis of AIZS-CDs-FcA can be proved from the above description.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and energy level structure of photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para:
Next, UV–vis absorption and PL spectroscopy tests were conducted on AIZS QDs, AIZS-FcA and AIZS-CDs-FcA. As shown in Fig. 2a, the light absorption of AIZS QDs and AIZS-FcA is almost the same, whereas the light absorption of AIZS-CDs-FcA is largely increased compared to AIZS QDs due to the contribution of CDs at long wavelengths. It can be seen from Fig. 2b that after the modification of AIZS QDs by FcA, the PL intensity is greatly reduced (3 times). And the PL intensity of the ternary AIZS-CDs-FcA hybrid is even lower (10.9 times lower than AIZS QDs). This shows that the introduction of CDs-FcA has a better ability to improve the separation efficiency of photo-generated charge carriers than FcA, as a strong evidence of the improved hole extraction capability. For band gap estimation, the diffuse reflectance spectrum (DRS) of AIZS QDs was conducted and converted into a Tauc plot (inset in Fig. 2c), and the optical band gap was calculated as follows:
(1)
αhν = A(hυ -Eg )2
In formula (1), the parameters are expressed separately: α (absorption coefficient), A (proportional constant), h (Planck constant), υ (frequency of the incident light) and Eg (band gap). The Tauc plot of (αhυ)2 vs. photon energy (hυ) are shown in the inset of Fig. 2c and the intersection of the tangent line and the abscissa represents the band gap energy, which is 2.03 eV for the AIZS QDs. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 2d, the XPS valence band spectrum of AIZS QDs gives the position of the valence band to be 1.07 eV by the intersection of the two tangents on the abscissa. Next, cyclic voltammetry was used to test the multiple energy levels of CDs to explore its wide-range hole accepting capability [61]. As shown in Fig. 2e, the position of the HOMO and LOMO of CDs were calculated by cyclic voltammetry. The potassium ferricyanide redox system was introduced as a standard. According to formula 2:
(2) EHOMO = − (−Efe + 4.80) eV

The energy level positions (Fig. 2e) of CDs were determined by cyclic voltammetry and the HOMO of CDs is the range of 1.03–1.60 eV. Finally, through the above tests, the energy band alignment of the composite system is summarized in Fig. 2f, where AIZS QDs shows conduction band of -0.97 eV and valence band of 1.07 eV, the HOMO of FcA is 0.67 eV (literature value) [62], and the HOMO of CDs is in the range between 1.03 eV and 1.60 eV. It can be seen from the energy level diagram that the multiple energy levels of the HOMO of CDs are slightly lower than the valence band of AIZS QDs and are suitable for mediating hole transfer thermodynamically. From this, it has been proved that CDs do possess the capability to transfer holes in this system. Therefore, it can be simply speculated that the photogenerated holes generated by AIZS QDs may be transferred to FcA through CDs with multiple energy levels and then undergo further redox reactions.

Next, the photocatalytic hydrogen production of the above three samples was tested under visible light using l-ascorbic acid (AA, 0.25 M) as the sacrificial agent, which intuitively shows that the introduction of CDs-FcA is even more conducive than FcA to the increase in photocatalytic hydrogen production of AIZS QDs. As shown in Fig. 3 a, pure CDs and FcA show almost no activity on hydrogen production, which proves that they do not have any independent activity, but only act as charge transfer mediating ligands. When CDs are combined with AIZS QDs, the total hydrogen production of AIZS-CDs reached 0.718 mmol g−1 after 5 h of reaction, with an hydrogen production rate of 0.287 mmol g−1 h−1(Fig S8). It can be seen that the total hydrogen production of the optimized AIZS-CDs-FcA composite photocatalyst reached 11.73 mmol g−1 after 5 h of reaction. From Fig. 3b, this AIZS-CDs-FcA sample exhibits the hydrogen production rate of 2.34 mmol g−1 h−1, which is 5.8 times higher than AIZS QDs and 2.9 times higher than AIZS-FcA. It can be seen that FcA, as a hole transfer material, was able to double the hydrogen production rate of AIZS QDs, but the result was not as high as expected. The main reason may be due to that the HOMO position of FcA (0.67 eV) differs greatly from the valence band position of AIZS QDs (1.07 eV), thus FcA cannot extract photogenerated holes of the QDs efficiently. The possible reason why AIZS-CDs reduces the catalytic activity is that CDs itself cannot efficiently transfer photo-generated holes. In addition, the CDs have high absorption in visible range and may block part of the light, which decreased the activity of AIZS-CDs. In contrast to the single HOMO position of FcA, CDs with multiple HOMO levels in the range of 1.03–1.6 eV (Fig. 2e), which together with larger contacting area provides much better opportunity for hole extraction in AIZS-CDs-FcA, resulting in 5.8 times increase of photocatalytic activity. The modification of FcA on CDs makes CDs have lower valence band positions and more active sites to react with the photogenerated holes, so CDs-FcA can effectively extract and consume holes. More importantly, besides CDs-FcA for hole extraction, we further introduced Ni2+ into AIZS-CDs-FcA as an electron extractor towards a synergetic enhancement of the photocatalytic hydrogen production. In the end, the optimized hydrogen production rate of AIZS-CDs-FcA/Ni reached 13.1 mmol g−1 h−1, 31.9 times higher than that of pure AIZS QDs after simultaneous electron and hole extraction, which is among one of the best results in the QDs-based photocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Activity optimization of the composite photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic activity of the AIZS-CDs-FcA composites was systematically investigated, especially the relative ratios of CDs, FcA, as well as Ni2+. The first factor is the total ratio of CDs-FcA over AIZS. When CDs-FcA is combined with AIZS QDs, the efficient hydrogen production activity can be obtained by the rapid extraction of holes. As shown in Fig. 4 a and b, the photocatalytic hydrogen production rate was observed to increase from 0.57 mmol g−1 h-1 (1%) to 1.20 mmol g−1 h-1 (4%) and then started to decrease to 0.73 mmol g−1 h-1(5%) with excessive CDs-FcA. The main reason is that the addition of CDs-FcA accelerates the extraction of holes, but excessive dark-colored CDs (>5%) may block the absorption of photons of AIZS and thus reduce the hydrogen production activity of the composite. It can be seen from the corresponding UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. S9a) that the absorbance of the samples rises obviously in the long wavelengths as the ratio of CDs increases. The PL spectra of the composites with CDs-FcA ratio also verifies the effective PL quenching when the ratio is 4% CDs-0.4 % FcA (Fig. S9c), an important evidence for the efficient charge separation. After this, the photocatalytic hydrogen production is optimized by changing the relative ratio of FcA over CDs, where the ratio of CDs was fixed at 4% (denoted as AIZS-4% CDs-X% FcA, Figs. 4c and d). As FcA increases, the hydrogen production rate of the composites gradually increases at first. As shown in Fig. 4d, as the amount of FcA increases, the rate of hydrogen production increases from 0.61 mmol g−1 h−1 (0.16 %) to the maximum of 2.34 mmol g-1 h−1 (0.64 %), and then decreases to 1.57 mmol g−1 h−1 (0.88 %). The possible main reason is that the increase of FcA enhances the hole extraction rate of AIZS QDs, but FcA itself also absorb part of the visible light and lead to the snatching of photons at excessive amount [63]. As shown in Fig. S9b, as the amount of FcA increases, the light absorption also increases. Through the joint systematic optimization of CDs and FcA, the best ratio of AIZS-CDs-FcA was determined to be AIZS-4% CDs-0.64 % FcA. It also can be proved by the PL quenching (Fig. S9d), where AIZS-4% CDs-0.64 % FcA has almost reached the lowest PL intensity, proving its efficient charge separation and subsequent hydrogen production performance. Finally, the charge separation ability was further improved by combining the catalyst with different amount of Ni2+ as an electron promoter. The synergetic electron and hole extraction immediately resulted in an order of enhancement of hydrogen production, which first increase with Ni2+ content and then started to decrease (Fig. 4e & f). When the Ni2+ content reaches 2.5 %, the hydrogen production rate reaches the highest value of 13.1 mmol g−1 h−1, resulting in a total of 31.9 times enhancement compared with pure AIZS QDs. AIZS-CDs-FcA with the introduction of overdose L-cysteine was also tested to rule out the impact of the initial capping ligand. As shown Fig. S10, excessive ligand addition leads to a decreased hydrogen production activity.

In order to more intuitively prove the critical role of CDs in the hole extraction of the composites, hole transfer rates of the three photocatalysts were studied and calculated via the PL lifetime from the time-resolved PL [21]. As shown in Fig. 5 a–e, the addition of the hole sacrificial agent (AA) can effectively lower the PL lifetime, where the variation of PL lifetime vs. concentrition of AA can be used to determine the hole transfer rates in the three photocatalysts. Charge transfer rate kct can be obtained from Eq. (3) [64].
(3) kct = (1⁄τ − 1⁄τ0)
τ and τ0 are the average lifetimes of QDs in the presence and absence of AA, respectively. When AA is added, it cangreatly inhibit PL of AIZS-FcA and AIZS-CDs-FcA. However, because the PL intensity of AIZS-CDs-FcA is very low, the decrease in emission of AIZS-CDs-FcA with the addition of AA is not as obvious as AIZS QDs (Fig. S11). In order to obtain the transfer rate of photo-generated holes in the three photocatalysts more accurately, a series of time-resolved PL tests were performed after adding different amount of the sacrificial agent AA. After AA was added to AIZS-CDs-FcA, it shows faster decay kinetics than AIZS QDs and AIZS-FcA (Fig. 5b, d and f, and Table S1 in Supporting Information). The fitting results show that upon the addition of AA, the average PL lifetime of AIZS QDs drops from 66.99 ns to 56.34 ns. The average lifetime of AIZS-FcA dropped from 47.61 ns to 25.26 ns. The average lifetime of AIZS-CDs-FcA dropped from 7.25 ns to 3.83 ns. Using Eq. (3), the hole transfer rates of all photocatalysts were calculated to be 1.11 × 108 s−1 mM−1, 7.28 × 108 s−1 mM−1 and 1.97 × 1010 s−1 mM−1, respectively. It is intuitively proved that CDs-FcA acts as a better hole extraction promoter than FcA to accelerate the hole transfer rate of AIZS QDs. Moreover, the addition of CDs increases the hole transfer rate by two orders of magnitude from 7.28 × 108 s−1 mM−1 (with FcA) to 1.97 × 1010 s−1 mM−1 (with CDs-FcA). Fig. S12 shows the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of AIZS QDs, AIZS-FcA and AIZS-CDs-FcA. It can be seen that the impedance radius of AIZS-CDs-FcA is smaller than the radius of AIZS QDs and AIZS-FcA, indicating that the charge transfer resistance of photo-generated charge carriers with CDs-FcA is smaller. It further proves the crucial role of CDs on the rapid transfer of holes.

Based on the above results, here we present a simple strategy for the fast-interfacial charge separation in the composite photocatalyst by using CDs as the hole extraction agent for the first time. With the introduction of CDs, the photocatalytic hydrogen production rate of AIZS-CDs-FcA composite catalyst reached 2.34 mmol g−1 h−1, which is 5.8 times that of AIZS QDs and 2.9 times of AIZS-FcA. When the electron accelerator (Ni2+) was further added, the hydrogen production rate was up to 13.1 mmol g−1 h−1 under synergistic hole/electron extraction, which was 31.9 times that of the unmodified QDs. This dramatic enhancement is ascribed to the improved charge migration and transportation as verified by further charge transfer tests. As shown in Fig. 6 , AIZS QDs formed two new composites after hydriding with FcA and CDs-FcA.

By time-resolved PL measurements, the hole transfer rate increased from 1.11 × 108 s−1 mM−1 to 7.28 × 108 s−1 mM−1 and 1.97 × 1010 s−1 mM−1 with the introduction of FcA and CDs-FcA, respectively. The impedance test further proves that AIZS-CDs-FcA has higher charge separation efficiency. The main factor for the two orders of increase of hole transfer rate in AIZS-CDs-FcA can be ascribed to the combined advantages of the multiple HOMO levels as indicated by cyclic voltammetry along with larger interfacial contact areas in CDs. CDs rapidly transfers photogenerated holes in AIZS QDs to FcA through appropriate energy level positions and greater contact area with AIZS QDs. As a result, the more photogenerated holes are extracted from the catalyst, the higher charge separation efficiency and photocatalytic activity can be obtained. In other words, the weaker PL intensity and shorter PL lifetime of AIZS-CDs-FcA are mainly due to the rapid extraction of holes in AIZS QDs by CDs-FcA and the transfer of holes to CDs-FcA to reduce the occurrence of electron-hole recombination. Since the valence band position of CDs is relatively close to that of AIZS QDs, CDs are mainly used as hole transfer intermediates, which can efficiently extract photo-generated holes in AIZS QDs and transfer them to FcA for further consumption. Therefore, the PL decay curves show lower radiative recombination and shorter lifetime. In order to prove the necessity of CDs-FcA as a precursor and the correctness of the hole transfer pathway proposed above, we also added pure CDs to AIZS-FcA, which only showed decreased photocatalytic hydrogen production (Fig. S13).

In this work, we demonstrate that CDs, as an efficient hole transfer mediator, work together with FcA to significantly boost photocatalytic hydrogen production of I-III-VI QDs. AIZS-CDs-FcA composite materials with high hole-extraction efficiency were synthesized by a simple two-step hydrothermal method. The photocatalytic hydrogen production rate of the AIZS-CDs-FcA composite was 5.8 times higher than that of AIZS QDs. By synergetic hole/electron extraction with introducing Ni2+, the hydrogen production rate was further increased to 13.1 mmol g−1 h−1, 31.9 times of pure AIZS QDs. More importantly, the mechanism was further proved by two orders of magnitude higher hole transfer rate of AIZS-CDs-FcA (1.97 × 1010 s−1 mM−1) than that of AIZS QDs (1.11 × 108 s−1 mM−1). This work provides a new way for using CDs as hole transfer intermediates in photocatalyst design. It also shines light on the new application of CDs as efficient hole transfer materials for photocatalysis as well as optoelectronic devices.
